retrieved_docs.title: Adrenalectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The right and left adrenal glands are located within Gerota fascia adjacent to the kidneys. The arterial supply of the adrenal glands consists of small arterial branches arising from the inferior phrenic artery, the aorta, and the renal arteries. Venous drainage of the left adrenal gland is to the left renal vein through the left adrenal vein, while the right adrenal vein drains directly into the inferior vena cava. The adrenal gland is comprised of two distinct layers, the outer adrenal cortex, and the inner adrenal medulla, which have distinct hormonal production. The adrenal medulla is made up of ectodermal cells of neural crest origin and is the site of catecholamine production, which includes dopamine, norepinephrine, and epinephrine.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Adrenal Gland -- Organ Systems Involved -- Adrenal Medulla and the Sympathetic Nervous System
retrieved_docs.content: The sympathetic nervous system regulates the secretion of epinephrine and norepinephrine from the adrenal medulla. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Adrenal Glands (Suprarenal Glands) -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The function of the adrenal gland is mediated by both synaptic stimulation and hormonal stimulation. Adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) secreted from the anterior pituitary gland activates the adrenal cortex. Subsequently, ACTH activates the respective cortical zones to generate corticosteroids. [3] [12] However, the adrenal medulla is innervated by preganglionic nerve fibers (type B) arising from the intermediolateral cell column of the spinal cord's lateral horn from the T5–T8 spinal cord segments. These nerve fibers form the greater splanchnic nerve without entering the paravertebral sympathetic ganglion chain. Some nerve fibers from the greater splanchnic nerve synapse at the celiac ganglion. The blood vessels supplying the adrenal glands then receive their innervation from the celiac ganglion's postsynaptic fibers.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Adrenal Glands (Suprarenal Glands) -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: On the other hand, some fibers of the greater splanchnic nerve circumvent the celiac ganglion and directly enter the adrenal gland to synapse at the chromaffin cells' membranes. This is why the adrenal medulla acts as a neuroendocrine junction between the 2 physiological segments. The chromaffin cells release their neurohormones directly into the bloodstream to produce a widespread sympathetic response and act as a special type of postsynaptic neuron. [3] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Adrenal Glands (Suprarenal Glands) -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: As the adrenal glands produce various systemically important hormones, they require significant blood supply and are extremely well vascularized. The blood supply is tightly controlled by neuroendocrine and paracrine mechanisms, which is 1 method of regulating the systemic levels of adrenal hormones. [5] The 3 chief sources of blood supply to the adrenal glands include: The superior adrenal arteries, which are small branches coming off the inferior phrenic artery The middle adrenal artery comes directly off the abdominal aorta. The inferior adrenal artery originates from the renal artery bilaterally [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Thorax, Greater Splanchnic Nerves -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The GSN also innervates the adrenal medulla's chromaffin cells, stimulating adrenaline (epinephrine) and noradrenaline (norepinephrine) secretion in response to stress. These hormones increase the heart rate and muscle blood flow, dilate the bronchioles, mobilize energy stores, and stimulate the brain's wakefulness centers.

retrieved_docs.title: Adrenal Hemorrhage -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The adrenal glands are a pair of endocrine organs located in the retroperitoneum on the upper poles of the kidneys. They comprise 2 embryologically and functionally distinct parts: the outer mesodermally derived adrenal cortex, which produces glucocorticoids, mineralocorticoids, and adrenal androgens, and the inner adrenal medulla, which originates from the neural crest and secretes catecholamines. The suprarenal glands are one of the most well-perfused organs in the human body. The superior, middle, and inferior adrenal arteries originate from the inferior phrenic artery, the abdominal aorta, and the renal artery, forming the chief arterial supply. In contrast, venous drainage is limited and often utilizes a single adrenal vein, which drains into the inferior vena cava on the right and the renal vein on the left. This difference between arterial inflow and venous drainage may explain the anatomic predisposition that leads to the development of hemorrhagic infarction. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Sympathetic Nervous System -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Actions of SANS which do not oppose those of the PANS include the following: There is strong constriction through the alpha-1 receptor in arterioles of the skin, abdominal viscera, and kidney, and weak constriction through the alpha-1 and beta-2 receptors in the skeletal muscle. In the liver, increased glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis (alpha-1, beta-2) occur to allow for glucose to be available for energy throughout the body. In the spleen, there is a contraction (alpha-1). Sweat glands and arrector pili muscles (muscarinic) work to increase sweating and erection of hair to help cool down the body. Lastly, the adrenal medulla (nicotinic receptor) increases the release of epinephrine and norepinephrine to act elsewhere in the body. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Adrenal Gland -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The adrenal gland is made up of the cortex and medulla. The cortex produces steroid hormones including glucocorticoids, mineralocorticoids, and adrenal androgens, and the medulla produces the catecholamines, epinephrine, and norepinephrine. This brief article reviews the physiology of the adrenal gland and highlights the relevance of understanding the clinical syndromes of excess and deficiency.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Adrenal Glands (Suprarenal Glands) -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The presence of ectopic adrenal tissue has been well documented. If performing a surgical resection of the adrenal gland, care is necessary to assess any ectopic tissue. Ectopic cortical tissue is often adjacent to the kidney or pelvis. Ectopic medullary tissue is located along the descending track of neural crest cells. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Adrenal Glands (Suprarenal Glands) -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: Variations in adrenal artery origin are very common. The superior adrenal artery can come off the abdominal aorta, celiac axis, or, more rarely, an intercostal artery. The superior adrenal artery is also commonly found as multiple arteries. The middle adrenal artery can come off the inferior phrenic, renal, or superior mesenteric arteries or the celiac axis. The inferior adrenal arteries can also originate from the abdominal aorta or the inferior phrenic artery. [7] [8] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Adrenal Glands (Suprarenal Glands) -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The adrenal medulla contains specialized cells known as chromaffin cells. These cells aggregate in small clusters around blood vessels and synthesize epinephrine and norepinephrine. These sympathetic hormones have many physiological activities, including playing a role in the "fight or flight" response, increasing heart rate, the force of contraction of the heart, metabolic rate, and cognitive awareness. For standardized test purposes, remember that multiple endocrine neoplasia-II is associated with pheochromocytoma and medullary thyroid cancer. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Autonomic Nervous System -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Epinephrine is released into systemic circulation by chromaffin cells within the adrenal gland, specifically the adrenal medulla. The sympathetic nervous system splanchnic nerves synapse on chromaffin cells and stimulate them to release epinephrine using the signal messenger acetylcholine. This feature of the adrenal gland is an important component of the sympathetic nervous system as it allows systemic effects to occur on major organ systems. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Adrenal Glands (Suprarenal Glands) -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: When removing a pheochromocytoma, a neuroendocrine tumor based on the adrenal medulla's chromaffin cells that produce large amounts of catecholamines, it is crucial to ligate the adrenal vein before manipulating the organ. If the adrenal vein is not ligated before manipulating the gland, the pheochromocytoma can release significant levels of catecholamines into the systemic circulation; this causes a catecholamine rush, resulting in a rapid onset of severe hypertension and tachycardia.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Adrenal Glands (Suprarenal Glands) -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The venous drainage from the adrenal glands is dependent on the side of the gland. The left adrenal gland is anatomically further away from the inferior vena cava; therefore, the left adrenal vein drains into the left renal vein. The right adrenal vein is much closer to the inferior vena cava and drains directly into this large vessel. Variations in adrenal venous drainage are common, particularly on the left side. There are reports of venous connections between the left adrenal vein, the left genital vein, and the inferior phrenic vein. Double left adrenal veins are also common. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Blood Thymus Barrier -- Nerves
retrieved_docs.content: The nerves that supply the thymus arise from the sympathetic chain and the vagus nerve. They enter the thymus within blood vessels and from perivascular plexes. [8] The phrenic nerve also supplies several branches that reach the thymic capsule but do not penetrate the parenchyma of the gland.

These ancillary ingredients are likely intended to supply nutritional support, antioxidant capacity, and additional pharmacologic actions that complement the primary adrenergic and corticotropic effects. The repeated pattern of pairing epinephrine with corticotropin, together with other endocrine hormones, neurotransmitters, minerals, vitamins, and herbal components, suggests a therapeutic strategy aimed at modulating sympathetic activity, adrenal cortical output, thyroid function, and overall metabolic homeostasis. The inclusion of both synthetic and natural substances across the products underscores a multimodal approach to patient (or animal) care that leverages synergistic actions of catecholamines, ACTH, and supportive nutrients to address conditions such as allergic reactions, neuro‑behavioral imbalances, weight management, and systemic endocrine deficiencies.

Together, these interconnections illustrate how thyroid‑targeted pharmacotherapy, thyroid gland pathology, head‑and‑neck surgical interventions, and middle‑ear procedures are organized within a unified medical framework, emphasizing anatomical sites, therapeutic categories, and procedural relationships relevant to comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 146
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenalin (epinephrine) is a catecholamine hormone synthesized in the adrenal medulla and classified within the adrenal and gonadal hormone family as an adaptive hormone. It exerts its effects by acting as an agonist at both α‑adrenergic and β‑adrenergic receptors, a pharmacologic property that underlies its inclusion in injectable solutions (e.g., 1 mL epinephrine 1 mg/mL) and various oral liquid formulations used in veterinary and human medicine. Adrenalin also serves as a parent compound for the metabolite normetanefrin; both molecules are linked to tumor biomarkers and are relevant in oncologic diagnostics. Within the same endocrine network, adrenal cortex hormones are organized hierarchically under the broader adrenal‑gonadal hormone class, alongside mineralocorticoids such as aldosterone and deoxycorticosterone (the latter chemically related to tetrahydrodeoxycorticosterone). These mineralocorticoids belong to the “Mineralocorticoids for systemic use, plain” subclass of systemic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1405
retrieved_docs.content: ADRENAL AND GONADAL HORMONES is a collective term that encompasses specific hormones such as aldosteron and also includes other endocrine substances classified as NOS (not otherwise specified), reflecting the broader range of hormonal products derived from the adrenal and gonadal glands.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2806
retrieved_docs.content: ADRENAL AND GONADAL HORMONES constitute a class of endocrine substances secreted by the adrenal cortex and the gonads, and aldosterone is identified as one of the hormones within this group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1321
retrieved_docs.content: The category “ADRENAL AND GONADAL HORMONES” encompasses several specific endocrine entities, including aldosteron, deoxykortikosteron, and a nonspecific class labeled “Endocrine substance, NOS.” Deoxykortikosteron is linked to the compound tetrahydrodeoxycorticosterone through a mapping relationship, indicating that deoxykortikosteron is derived from or closely related to tetrahydrodeoxycorticosterone. In addition, the entity “Adrenal medulla hormone (substance)” also lists “Endocrine substance, NOS” as a child, showing that this nonspecific endocrine substance is shared between the broader adrenal and gonadal hormone system and the adrenal medulla secretions. Together, these relationships illustrate how specific hormones such as aldosteron and deoxykortikosteron fit within the larger framework of adrenal and gonadal endocrine activity, while the generic “Endocrine substance, NOS” serves as a common classification for unnamed or miscellaneous endocrine products produced by both the adrenal cortex and medulla. Understanding these hierarchical connections aids clinicians in recognizing the origins and classifications of various adrenal‑related hormones and substances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 52
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenalin (epinephrine) is positioned as a primary catecholamine hormone within the hierarchical group “ADRENAL AND GONADAL HORMONES,” which also contains aldosteron, deoxykortikosteron, and the generic entity Endocrine substance, NOS; aldosteron and deoxykortikosteron are linked to the pharmaceutical class “Mineralocorticoids for systemic use, plain,” connecting these mineralocorticoid hormones to systemic therapy. Deoxykortikosteron is biochemically derived from tetrahydrodeoxycorticosterone, and the adrenal medulla hormone (substance) lists adrenalin as its parent, reinforcing adrenalin’s role as the central catecholamine of adrenal‑medullary secretion. Adrenalin serves as an active ingredient in numerous oral‑liquid and injectable products, including complex formulations such as “CORTICOTROPIN 6 [hp_X] … EPINEPHRINE 6 [hp_X] …,” “EPINEPHRINE 10 [hp_X] …,” and commercial preparations like Neurobalance Pro, OHM Pituitarum Complex, Appetite and Weight Natural Pet, FCS HP, MBM 11

retrieved_docs.content: adrenergic receptors. Collectively, these relationships portray adrenalin as a central endocrine hormone derived from the adrenal medulla, widely employed as an active ingredient in multifaceted oral liquid preparations, and integral to adrenergic receptor pharmacology and adaptive hormonal pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1263
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenalin, also known as epinephrine, is the principal active ingredient in several pharmaceutical preparations. It is the key component of the 1 mL epinephrine 1 mg/mL Injectable Solution, as well as of the oral liquid formulations identified as the OHM Pituitarum Complex (which contains EPINEPHRINE 10 [hp_X] among many other hormonal and tissue extracts) and the HP Axis oral liquid (which lists EPINEPHRINE 8 [hp_X] together with a wide array of mineral, botanical, and hormonal substances). In addition to its role as a drug ingredient, adrenalin is classified among adaptive hormones, reflecting its function in the body’s rapid stress‑response mechanisms. Its physiological actions are mediated through adrenergní receptory (adrenergic receptors), which transmit the catecholamine’s effects on cardiovascular, respiratory, and metabolic systems. Together, these relationships underscore adrenalin’s importance both as a therapeutic agent in emergency and hormonal preparations and as a central mediator of the body’s adaptive stress response.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1559
retrieved_docs.content: The endocrine system (endokrinní systém) is directly implicated as the procedural site for both an Endocrine system incision and a Radioisotope study of endocrine system, indicating that these interventions are performed on the endocrine organs themselves. In addition, the endocrine system is identified as the target anatomy for a castration (kastrace) procedure, meaning that castration interventions are directed toward structures within the endocrine system. Together, these relationships underscore the endocrine system’s central role as both a focus for diagnostic and surgical procedures and a specific anatomical target for interventions that alter its hormonal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 155
retrieved_docs.content: illustrates that the thymus, an endocrine organ, can serve as a metastatic destination. Renal oncocytoma (الورم الأورمي الكلوي) and renal cell carcinoma (سرطان الخلايا الكلوية) are specific entities within cancer of the kidney and renal pelvis; partial or complete nephrectomy (استئصال الكلية) is the surgical intervention commonly associated with these renal tumors. Collectively, these interrelated concepts demonstrate that malignant cellular infiltrates and malignant or uncertain tumor cells define primary and secondary (metastatic) disease across multiple organ systems—lymph nodes, bone, skin, respiratory and intrathoracic organs, endocrine glands, thymus, breast, ovary, kidney, and pancreas—providing clinicians with a structured framework for cancer origin identification, staging, and targeted therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 971
retrieved_docs.content: The aorta is a major arterial structure whose anatomy and histology are fundamental topics for understanding its form and function, and its growth and development are recognized as important aspects of cardiovascular physiology. In clinical contexts, the aorta is also a permissible focus for evaluating drug effects, reflecting the relevance of pharmacologic influences on this vessel. Surgical interventions such as aortic resection, replacement, or anastomosis are performed directly on the aorta, making it the direct procedural site for these operations. Likewise, hormones that inhibit the release of pituitary hormones are identified as a category for which drug effects may be examined, indicating that pharmacologic considerations extend to endocrine regulators as well as vascular structures. Together, these relationships highlight how the aorta, its developmental biology, its anatomical‑histological characteristics, and the pharmacology of both vascular and hormonal systems are interconnected in medical assessment and patient care.
